title: Cartan Fluxes in $SU(3)$ Lattice Gauge Theory
summary: We propose and analyze a new method of detecting center vortices and monopoles in lattice Yang-Mills theory. This procedure is sensitive to the intrinsic degeneracy of the center charges, which play a crucial role in how these topological objects interact and correlate with one another. Our approach is based on fixing the Maximal Abelian gauge (MAG) and decomposing the link configuration in a suitable way to look for so-called Cartan fluxes. Our discussion is general for $SU(N)$ gauge theory, but we focus our applications on the $SU(3)$ case. For the $SU(2)$ case, our proposed parametrization is equivalent to the usual one.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a novel method for detecting center vortices and monopoles in lattice Yang-Mills theory, which is particularly sensitive to the intrinsic degeneracy of center charges. It uses Maximal Abelian gauge (MAG) fixing and link configuration decomposition to identify Cartan fluxes, with applications focused on SU(3) gauge theory while also being applicable to SU(2).
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this research is to improve the detection and understanding of topological objects such as center vortices and monopoles in lattice Yang-Mills theory, by taking into account the intrinsic degeneracy of center charges that influence their interactions and correlations.
AI.method: The authors propose a method that involves fixing the Maximal Abelian gauge (MAG) and decomposing the link configuration to detect Cartan fluxes, which are indicative of the presence of center vortices and monopoles. The method is formulated to be general for SU(N) gauge theories, with particular emphasis on the SU(3) case, and it is noted that for SU(2) the parametrization matches the conventional one.
AI.result: The results of the study, although not explicitly stated in the abstract, would likely consist of a more refined and accurate detection of center vortices and monopoles within the lattice framework, providing new insights into the role of center charge degeneracy in their behavior and interaction.
AI.conclusion: While the conclusion is not provided in the abstract, the paper presumably concludes that the proposed method offers a valuable tool for studying the dynamics of center vortices and monopoles, potentially leading to a better understanding of their impact on the properties of Yang-Mills theories.

title: Impact of supersymmetry on the dynamical emergence of the spacetime in the type IIB matrix model with the Lorentz symmetry "gauge fixed"
comment: 10 pages, 3 figures, presented at the 42nd International Symposium on Lattice Field Theory (LATTICE 2025), 2-8 November, Mumbai, India
summary: The type IIB matrix model has been proposed as a nonperturbative formulation of superstring theory. While numerical simulations of this model are essential for probing nonperturbative effects, such as the emergence of time and an expanding 3--dimensional space, they are hindered by the sign problem. We address this using the Complex Langevin Method (CLM). Furthermore, to suppress spurious numerical artifacts that originate from large Lorentz boosts due to the Lorentz symmetry of the model, we nonperturbatively fix the Lorentz symmetry using the Faddeev--Popov procedure. We then study this model to investigate the impact of supersymmetry on the dynamical generation of (3+1)--dimensional spacetime.

AI.tldr: The paper uses the Complex Langevin Method to address the sign problem in type IIB matrix model simulations and applies the Faddeev--Popov procedure to fix Lorentz symmetry, aiming to investigate supersymmetry's impact on the generation of (3+1)-dimensional spacetime.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to overcome the challenges in simulating the type IIB matrix model, a nonperturbative formulation of superstring theory, specifically the sign problem and numerical artifacts from Lorentz boosts, to better understand the emergence of time and 3D space as well as the role of supersymmetry.
AI.method: The authors use the Complex Langevin Method (CLM) to tackle the sign problem and employ the Faddeev--Popov procedure to nonperturbatively fix the Lorentz symmetry, thereby stabilizing the simulation and reducing spurious effects.
AI.result: The result is a more stable and controlled computational framework for studying the type IIB matrix model, which allows for a clearer investigation into the dynamical generation of (3+1)-dimensional spacetime and the influence of supersymmetry on this process.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion would be that by addressing the sign problem and fixing Lorentz symmetry, the study provides a more robust approach to simulate the type IIB matrix model, which could lead to new insights into the nonperturbative aspects of superstring theory, including the role of supersymmetry in the formation of spacetime.

title: Determination of heavy meson light-cone distribution amplitudes: theoretical framework and lattice simulations
summary: We present a first-principles determination of heavy meson light-cone distribution amplitudes (LCDAs) from lattice QCD in the continuum limit, improving substantially on our previous pioneering study. Within the heavy-quark large-momentum effective theory (HQLaMET) framework, supplemented by lattice QCD calculations of the OPE moments, we analyze six ensembles with lattice spacings ranging from $a=0.0519-0.1053$\,fm and pion masses from $m_π=135.5-317.2$\,MeV, thereby enabling controlled continuum, chiral, and infinite-momentum extrapolations to the physical point. Momentum-smeared sources, hypercubic-smeared Wilson lines, and optimized interpolating operators are adopted to significantly improved signals for the nonlocal correlators. Within a unified framework, we determine both QCD LCDAs and HQET LCDAs. Our resulting QCD LCDAs of $D$ meson peak at $y\approx 0.2-0.3$, with total uncertainties below $30\%$ for $0.1<y<0.9$. The leading-twist HQET LCDA is constructed using a peak-and-tail factorization, in which the nonperturbative peak region is obtained from lattice QCD and the perturbative tail is incorporated from HQET, with the two regions combined through a model-independent Laguerre-polynomial parametrization. At $μ=1$\,GeV, we obtain the inverse moment of HQET LCDA $λ_B=0.340(20)$\,GeV and first inverse-logarithmic moment $σ_B^{(1)}=1.685(63)$, consistent with experimental constraints and phenomenological determinations. Direct lattice calculations based on operator product expansion provide a nontrivial cross-check of the LaMET results. Final results and phenomenological impact of these results are presented in a companion paper~\cite{HeavymesonDA_short_paper}. Our results remove the single-lattice-spacing limitation of the previous study, and provide a robust determinations of heavy meson LCDAs in both QCD and HQET for next-generation heavy flavor physics.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an advanced determination of heavy meson light-cone distribution amplitudes (LCDAs) using lattice QCD, achieving the continuum limit and significantly improving upon previous work. The study employs the HQLaMET framework, lattice QCD calculations, and various techniques to improve signal quality. It provides both QCD and HQET LCDAs with uncertainties below 30% for a specific range and is consistent with experimental constraints.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve the accuracy and robustness of heavy meson light-cone distribution amplitudes (LCDAs) determinations by overcoming the limitations of previous single-lattice-spacing studies, and to provide reliable inputs for next-generation heavy flavor physics experiments and theoretical models.
AI.method: The method involves using the heavy-quark large-momentum effective theory (HQLaMET) framework, combined with lattice QCD calculations of OPE moments, on six ensembles with varying lattice spacings and pion masses. Momentum-smeared sources, hypercubic-smeared Wilson lines, and optimized interpolating operators are used to enhance the signals. Both QCD and HQET LCDAs are determined within a unified framework, and the HQET LCDA is further analyzed using peak-and-tail factorization with a Laguerre-polynomial parametrization.
AI.result: The results include QCD LCDAs for D mesons with a peak at approximately y=0.2-0.3 and total uncertainties below 30% for 0.1<y<0.9. For HQET, the inverse moment of the LCDA λ_B and the first inverse-logarithmic moment σ_B^(1) are obtained, which are in agreement with experimental data and other phenomenological determinations. The findings also provide a nonperturbative check of the LaMET results through direct lattice calculations based on the operator product expansion.
AI.conclusion: The conclusions drawn from this research are that the new approach has successfully removed the single-lattice-spacing limitation of prior studies and has provided more accurate and robust determinations of heavy meson LCDAs in both QCD and HQET. These determinations will be valuable for future heavy flavor physics studies, as they offer a solid foundation for both theoretical and experimental advancements.
